target: Section 6.2 (The Eclipse Test: Not What It Appears) in data/sections.json part6
replacement_html:
6.2 The Eclipse Test: Not What It Appears

The August 12, 2026 Eclipse Test is presented as the single most important discriminating prediction. The dome registers 9 per-station predictions across two baselines (BOU at −10.9 nT and W004 at −22.24 nT), producing a combined prediction range of approximately −5 to −26 nT with error bars. Four model-discriminating sub-tests (E-PRED-A through D) are registered, of which E-PRED-B (Hartland exceeds Ebro despite lower coverage) is the only one not trivially predicted by standard ionospheric physics. However, the dual-baseline structure covers nearly the entire physically plausible quiet-day eclipse signal space — the Chapman ionospheric mechanism (peer-reviewed since 1933) predicts 5–25 nT under identical conditions, making the BOU baseline predictions entirely non-discriminating and the W004 baseline predictions overlapping at most stations. The W004 baseline itself was an "empirical recalibration" from a prior failed weekly test (v50.2), casting doubt on its independence. See Section 3.2 for the full analysis.

The dome's monitor.py hardcodes a Kp < 2 precondition: if geomagnetic activity is elevated on eclipse day, the test automatically records pass=null rather than pass=false. While quiet-condition filtering is standard in geomagnetic research, the concern is whether pass/fail criteria and Kp data sources are committed in advance — or whether the dome reserves discretion to retroactively reclassify conditions after seeing unfavorable results. This is the dome's third eclipse test after two prior attempts that produced inconclusive results (2024 eclipse contaminated by storm activity, PROS-003 suspended for uncorrelated Z minima) — each followed by a refined formula and wider acceptance criteria.

A further internal inconsistency: the dome's monitor.py computes the eclipse prediction as −18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ −28.9 nT, using KAPPA (the magnetic-gravity coupling constant from WIN-012) as the field strength factor. But the dome's predictions page lists station-specific FSF values ranging from 0.642 to 2.075, derived from "V12 H(r)/r dome geometry." The monitoring infrastructure uses a different formula from the registered predictions — the code that will judge pass/fail does not implement the prediction it is supposed to test.

We commit publicly: if August 2026 measurements fall in the W004 range but outside the Chapman mechanism's expected range, and E-PRED-B is confirmed in a pattern inconsistent with standard Sq current geometry, we will revisit this verdict. We apply the same pre-commitment standard to ourselves that we apply to the dome.
holes_addressed: ISS-428: Replaced stale '-17 to -21 nT' with accurate dual-baseline description (BOU -5 to -13 nT, W004 -10 to -26 nT)

ISS-429: Replaced unverifiable '-29.1 nT' with verified monitor.py computation (-18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ -28.9 nT) and the FSF discrepancy between monitor.py (uses KAPPA 1.672) and predictions page (station-specific FSFs 0.642-2.075)
ISS-430: E-PRED tests now acknowledged in paragraph 1, with E-PRED-B identified as the only non-trivially-predicted sub-test
Curmudgeon minor hole: Kp escape clause now qualified with 'standard in geomagnetic research' acknowledgment
Curmudgeon stronger_argument #2: W004 provenance (failed weekly test → repurposed baseline) now mentioned
Curmudgeon stronger_argument #3: Third eclipse test attempt now mentioned with prior failure history
new_evidence_added: Verified monitor.py computation: ecm_eclipse = -18.22 * 0.95 * 1.672 ≈ -28.9 nT (actual code, not claimed)
FSF discrepancy: monitor.py uses KAPPA (1.672) as FSF while predictions page uses station-specific values (0.642-2.075)
Third-attempt pattern: 2024 eclipse storm contamination → PROS-003 suspended → 2026 with wider criteria
anticipated_objections: Dome may claim monitor.py uses a simplified all-station average while the predictions page has per-station detail — but this means the code that judges pass/fail doesn't implement the stated prediction
Dome may argue W004 baseline was a legitimate empirical correction — but the provenance chain (failed test → baseline) still undermines independence
Dome may argue Kp filtering is standard practice — we now acknowledge this while noting the retroactive-discretion concern
kernel_of_truth: The eclipse prediction is the most detailed and best-documented element of the dome model. The pre-registration is genuine, the station selection is reasonable, and E-PRED-B (Hartland > Ebro despite lower coverage) is a non-trivial relative prediction that standard physics doesn't trivially guarantee. The dual-baseline approach, while it creates hedging, also shows genuine engagement with observational uncertainty. The dome deserves credit for making a specific, falsifiable prediction with error bars — something most alternative cosmologies never do.

verification_notes.monitor_py_line: ecm_eclipse = -18.22 * 0.95 * 1.672 (line ~549 of monitor.py, fetched via raw GitHub URL 2026-04-08)
verification_notes.monitor_py_result: -28.93 nT (rounds to -29.0, not exactly -29.1 as we previously claimed — corrected to ≈ -28.9)
verification_notes.fsf_discrepancy_detail: monitor.py uses single FSF=1.672 (KAPPA). Predictions page lists per-station FSFs: EBR 1.029, SPT 1.001, ESK 1.722, LER 2.075, HAD 1.471, NGK 1.529, SNK 0.642, CLF 1.325, COI 1.008
verification_notes.inject_ai_layer_detail: inject_ai_layer.py shows delta_Z = baseline * coverage_fraction * latitude_factor formula with BOU-based predictions only (PRED-001 through PRED-005), ranging -5.8 to -9.5 nT. Uses 'latitude_factor' (not FSF), and magnetic_gravity_coupling=1.67 is listed as a core parameter but not used in the eclipse formula. Third formula variant.
verification_notes.formula_inconsistency_summary: Three different eclipse formulas across the dome's own codebase: (1) monitor.py: -18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672, (2) predictions.html: baseline × coverage × FSF with per-station FSFs, (3) inject_ai_layer.py: baseline × coverage_fraction × latitude_factor with different station set. The model that claims 95.2% accuracy cannot even maintain a consistent formula for its flagship prediction.

note_on_misrouted_file: EXP-005.json contains EXP-008 (taxonomy) content from a previous misrouted run, similar to EXP-004.json containing SEC-4.8 content. This file (EXP-005-sec62.json) contains the actual SEC-6.2 expansion.
